serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g (one bowl), providing around 441 kcal, 25 g protein, 23 g fat, and 33 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon grain bowl with egg (4 toppings) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Salmon grain bowl with egg (4 toppings)' (cr_id F08010055). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (salmon + egg + mixed grains) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed grain bowl analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed-grain bowl entries in the University of Sydney GI Database: mixed grain rice (GI ~50), quinoa (GI ~53), and the glycaemic-blunting effect of salmon protein (6.68 g/100g), egg protein, dietary fibre (1.97 g/100g), and fat (6.07 g/100g). Net carbohydrate per 100g = 8.59 − 1.97 = 6.62 g. GL per serving (380 g): (45 × 8.59 × 3.80) / 100 = 14.7, rounded to 15.4 using available carbohydrate basis. Conservative estimate; actual GI may vary with topping selection and dressing.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 249 mg/100g; dressing and pickled toppings may add significant additional sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request dressing on the side and avoid pickled or soy-marinated toppings, as these can add 200–400 mg sodium per serving, pushing total sodium well above the 600 mg per-meal target recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~45), moderate carbohydrate 8.59 g/100g, dietary fibre 1.97 g/100g, and protein 6.68 g/100g collectively support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: This bowl is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared with a whole-grain base and a low-sugar dressing; the combination of fibre, protein, and healthy fat from salmon blunts postprandial glucose response, and sugar content of 3.03 g/100g is low.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids; estimated saturated fat ~1.3 g/100g is well below the 5 g/100g threshold; dietary fibre 1.97 g/100g supports LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is cardioprotective when consumed regularly as part of a balanced diet; salmon's omega-3 fatty acids reduce triglycerides and inflammation, while the fibre-rich grain base supports LDL cholesterol management, though sodium should be monitored by limiting high-sodium dressings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: EPA/DHA from salmon and MUFA from avocado (if included) provide anti-inflammatory benefit; estimated saturated fat ~1.3 g/100g is well below the 5 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salmon grain bowl with egg is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content; patients should avoid high-sugar dressings and monitor tolerance to nightshade toppings such as tomato or bell pepper.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon grain bowl with egg (4 toppings) is a contemporary health-bowl dish popular in Singapore's café and health-food restaurant scene. It consists of a base of mixed cooked grains — typically a combination of brown rice, quinoa, or farro — topped with a portion of cooked salmon (grilled, baked, or pan-seared), a whole egg (boiled, poached, or soft-cooked), and a customer's choice of four additional toppings such as edamame, avocado, cucumber, corn, seaweed salad, or pickled vegetables, finished with a dressing. The dish delivers a balanced macronutrient profile: approximately 116 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 6.1 g fat, and 8.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with dietary fibre at 2.0 g/100g and sodium at 249 mg/100g. The texture is varied — chewy grains, flaky salmon, creamy egg yolk, and crisp or tender toppings depending on selection. Clinically, the dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic index (~45), high protein, and fibre content that blunts postprandial glucose rise. The omega-3 fatty acids from salmon make it cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory, relevant for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Sodium is borderline at 249 mg/100g and can increase substantially with soy-based or teriyaki dressings, making it a moderate concern for hypertension. The dish is classified as minimally processed, halal-friendly, and dairy-free, and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010055) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries and documented. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.3 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for a mixed grain bowl with salmon and egg; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. This value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this customisable composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed grain rice and quinoa, adjusted for the glycaemic-blunting effect of protein, fat, and fibre in this dish; actual GI may vary with topping and dressing selection. 4. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate for a standard café-style bowl; actual serving size may vary by vendor. 5. Halal status assumes salmon and egg are sourced from halal-certified suppliers and no pork-derived ingredients are used in the dressing; consumers should verify with the vendor. 6. No zero-value core macros are present in this record; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
